metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` must be updated to add the `autolander-gerrit-token` leaf (and its env-var mapping, e.g. `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN`) to its hardcoded fetch list — the script does not dynamically read all `/rebar/prod/*` leaves; each must be added manually.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the autolander Gerrit HTTP token is 'fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`', but reading `fetch-secrets.sh` shows it has a hardcoded leaf list (lines 81–91 of the file) that does NOT include `autolander-gerrit-token`. The script must be explicitly modified to add the new leaf — this is not covered by existing code.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC2, replace 'fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`' with 'fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` (add `autolander-gerrit-token → AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN` to the script's hardcoded leaf list at lines 81–91 and the corresponding output line)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` to the plan's explicit edit-target list, specifying the new `get_param autolander-gerrit-token` call and the corresponding `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN=...` line in the generated `.env`.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` is named as the fetch mechanism for `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token` but is not listed as an edit target. The file currently has a fixed set of `get_param` calls and `.env` write lines; adding the new SSM leaf requires a concrete code addition to this file.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line in the plan's edit-targets: 'Edit `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`: add `autolander_gerrit_token="$(get_param autolander-gerrit-token)"` and `echo "AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN=${autolander_gerrit_token}"` to the .env write block.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in AC3/AC4 that the HEALTHCHECK CMD must explicitly write a structured marker (e.g. `echo AUTOLANDER_UNHEALTHY >&2`) to stderr on failure so it appears in journald and can be grepped by `observability.sh`; or alternatively, specify that the observability probe polls `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}'` for the unhealthy state instead of relying on journald.
findings.finding: The 'unhealthy' container state feeder described in AC3 relies on grepping journald (`CONTAINER_NAME=compose-autolander-1`) for the `unhealthy` state, but Docker's health-check state is a daemon-side flag — it does NOT appear as a log line in journald unless the HEALTHCHECK CMD explicitly writes a structured marker to stderr on failure. Without that specification, the observability.sh journald-grep pattern (used for VOTER_ERROR, MERGE_CHANGE_ERROR, etc.) cannot detect the 'unhealthy' state, leaving the second feeder of the autolander_errors metric silent during container hangs.
findings.suggested_fix: In the HEALTHCHECK spec (AC4), add: 'The HEALTHCHECK CMD must emit a structured stderr line (e.g. `echo AUTOLANDER_UNHEALTHY >&2`) on failure so the journald-grep feeder in observability.sh can detect it; or change the unhealthy-state feeder in observability.sh to poll `docker inspect --format {{.State.Health.Status}} compose-autolander-1` and publish 1 when the result is "unhealthy".' Either approach closes the gap; the plan must pick one and spec it explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the `unhealthy`-restart mechanism concrete in AC4: either name the autoheal sidecar image and its required compose configuration (Docker socket mount, `AUTOHEAL_CONTAINER_LABEL` filter) as an explicit deliverable, or specify an alternative such as a host-side systemd timer that calls `docker inspect` + `docker restart`.
findings.finding: AC4 specifies 'autoheal/`restart: unless-stopped`' to restart the container on `unhealthy`, but does not specify what 'autoheal' means concretely. The existing docker-compose.yml has no autoheal sidecar, and plain `docker compose` (non-Swarm) `restart: unless-stopped` only restarts on process EXIT — not on Docker health state `unhealthy`. An autoheal sidecar (e.g. willfarrell/autoheal, which calls `docker restart` on `unhealthy` containers) would need to be added as a new service in docker-compose.yml, but neither its image, configuration, nor compose service definition is called out as a deliverable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` marker's emission contract: specify (a) which conditions in the loop service emit it (e.g., failed submit, unhandled exception, N=5 re-drive exhaustion), (b) the exact string/format written to stderr, and (c) the grep pattern that observability.sh will use — either in this plan's AC3 or as an explicit interface note back to S2.
findings.finding: The `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` journald marker is referenced in AC3 as one of two feeders to the `autolander_errors` CloudWatch metric, but its emission contract is never defined: no code location in loop.py/the service specifies when to write it, no grep pattern is given for observability.sh, and the marker does not exist anywhere in the current codebase or in the S2 dependency ticket (0ee8-08de-bf0f-45ec). An implementer of this story cannot wire the observability feeder without inventing the marker definition.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'The loop service writes `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` to stderr on [list conditions, e.g., unhandled exception, submit failure after N retries]; `observability.sh` greps `CONTAINER_NAME=compose-autolander-1` for `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` using the same offset-delta pattern as section 4.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for the `unhealthy`-state feeder in observability.sh: either (a) explain how the HEALTHCHECK failure appears in journald under CONTAINER_NAME=compose-autolander-1 (e.g., via autoheal writing a log line), or (b) specify an alternative source (`docker inspect`/`docker events`) and the resulting implementation pattern.
findings.finding: AC3 specifies a two-feeder `autolander_errors` metric (AUTOLANDER_ERROR journald grep + container `unhealthy` state) but does not specify how the `unhealthy` container state is observable in journald. Unlike the AUTOLANDER_ERROR feeder (stdout/stderr → journald), Docker health-check state transitions (`unhealthy`) appear in `docker events` output, not in the container's journald stream under CONTAINER_NAME. The plan says both feeders go through `infra/scripts/observability.sh` but gives no implementation protocol for the `unhealthy` feeder, unlike every peer section in observability.sh which fully specifies its source and pattern.
findings.location: AC3 (Observability): 'the container's unhealthy state (grepped on CONTAINER_NAME=compose-autolander-1)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC3's `unhealthy`-state feeder: replace the journald-grep approach with a mechanism that actually observes Docker health state — e.g., `docker inspect --format='{{.State.Health.Status}}' compose-autolander-1` in observability.sh, or a `docker events --filter event=health_status` watcher — and update AC3 to name the concrete detection method.
findings.finding: The plan's AC3 observability mechanism for the `unhealthy`-state feeder is technically infeasible as described. AC3 states the `unhealthy` container state is detected by observability.sh 'grepped on CONTAINER_NAME=compose-autolander-1'. The existing observability.sh uses `journalctl CONTAINER_NAME=<name>` to scan container stdout/stderr log lines. Docker health-state transitions (`healthy` → `unhealthy`) are emitted as Docker daemon events via `docker events`, NOT as stdout/stderr lines shipped to journald under `CONTAINER_NAME=`. journald will not contain an `unhealthy` token from a Docker health state change; it will only contain what the container's HEALTHCHECK command writes to stdout/stderr.
findings.suggested_fix: In observability.sh, add a stanza that calls `docker inspect --format='{{.State.Health.Status}}' compose-autolander-1 2>/dev/null` and publishes `autolander_errors += 1` when the result is `unhealthy`. This is synchronous, uses the already-present Docker CLI, and requires no daemon-socket sidecar. Update AC3 to specify `docker inspect` as the unhealthy-state detection method.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC4: Clarify the unhealthy-restart mechanism — if autoheal (willfarrell/autoheal) is the intended tool, add it as an explicit new compose service with justification; if the intent is to avoid a new dependency, specify an alternative (e.g., a wrapper entrypoint that exits the process on hang timeout, allowing restart:unless-stopped to handle it, or use `docker run --restart=on-failure` semantics).
findings.finding: AC4 mentions 'autoheal/restart:unless-stopped' as the mechanism to restart the container on unhealthy state, but 'autoheal' refers to a third-party container (willfarrell/autoheal) that has zero existing call-sites anywhere in this codebase or compose stack. Introducing it would add a new external dependency without established precedent; alternatively, if the intent is only restart:unless-stopped (which does NOT fire on unhealthy, only on process exit), the hang-detection HEALTHCHECK goal is unmet.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart): 'an autoheal/restart: unless-stopped recreate it on exit/unhealthy'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the ambiguous 'autoheal/restart:unless-stopped' phrasing in AC4 with one of: (a) 'add a willfarrell/autoheal sidecar service to docker-compose.yml that watches HEALTHCHECK state and docker-restarts unhealthy containers' (with explicit justification for the new dependency); or (b) 'the HEALTHCHECK command exits 1 on hang AND the entrypoint wraps the main process with a timeout that causes a process exit, so restart:unless-stopped alone handles both crash and hang recovery' (no new dependency).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC2: Specify the `fetch-secrets.sh` update — add `autolander-gerrit-token -> AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN` to the `get_param` call list and the `.env` write block, matching the pattern used for every other container secret.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the autolander Gerrit token is 'fetched by infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh' but does not specify adding `autolander-gerrit-token` to the script's curated SSM-leaf → env-var mapping. The existing script has an explicit, closed list of `get_param` calls and a matching `.env` write block; without a concrete addition, the SSM SecureString at `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token` will exist in Terraform but will never be read or written to the container's `.env`, leaving the container without its credential at runtime.
findings.location: AC2 (Credentials) — 'fetched by infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh'; also infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh lines 81–108
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's AC2 paragraph, add: 'Also add `autolander_gerrit_token="$(get_param autolander-gerrit-token)"` to `fetch-secrets.sh` (required, not optional) and `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN=${autolander_gerrit_token}` to the `.env` write block.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named provisioning artifact for the `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` capability grant — either extend `infra/gerrit/setup-project.sh` (or a new `infra/gerrit/setup-autolander-capability.sh`) with a git push to `All-Projects` `refs/meta/config` adding the `[capability]` section, or add it to an operator runbook — and reference that artifact in AC2.
findings.finding: The plan names `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` as a server-level capability to be granted in `All-Projects refs/meta/config [capability]`, but does not specify any provisioning artifact (script, operator runbook, or Terraform resource) that actually adds this grant. The existing repo has service-user.sh for the rebar project's webhooks.config and setup-project.sh for the rebar project's refs/meta/config — neither touches All-Projects. There is no existing script that edits All-Projects refs/meta/config, and adding a capability there requires admin SSH access and a separate refs/meta/config push to the All-Projects repository.
findings.location: AC2 (Credentials) — '`rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` is a server-level capability granted in `All-Projects` `refs/meta/config` (`[capability]` / service-user grant)'

findings.finding: The plan states the Gerrit HTTP token is 'fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`', but the existing script hard-codes `SSM_PREFIX="/rebar/prod"` and fetches a fixed, named set of leaf parameters — there is no autolander entry and no mechanism to fetch a parameter outside `/rebar/prod/`. The plan does not specify that the script must be extended.

The plan asserts that `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` is granted as a `[capability]` stanza in `All-Projects` `refs/meta/config`, but no existing script or config in the repo provisions `All-Projects` capabilities — only the `rebar` project's `refs/meta/config` is managed (via `setup-project.sh`). The exact config key for this capability on Gerrit 3.14.1 and the delivery script/mechanism are both unspecified, leaving the capability grant unverified and undelivered.

The plan states the Gerrit HTTP token is 'fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`', but `fetch-secrets.sh` hardcodes `SSM_PREFIX='/rebar/prod'` and only reads a fixed set of named leaves under that prefix. The path `/rebar/autolander/gerrit-http-token` will never be fetched by the current script — it is silently absent from the generated `.env`, so the container starts without the credential even if the IAM scope issue is separately resolved.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `autolander_gerrit_http_token="$(get_param autolander-gerrit-http-token)"` to the fetch block in `fetch-secrets.sh` and `echo "AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_HTTP_TOKEN=${autolander_gerrit_http_token}"` to the `.env` write block. Then in `docker-compose.yml`'s autolander service, add `env_file: - .env` (matching the review-bot pattern).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC2: declare the exact env-var name (e.g. `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN`) the SSM leaf `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token` maps to in `.env`, and explicitly state that `fetch-secrets.sh` is updated to include this leaf-to-var binding.
findings.finding: The plan names fetch-secrets.sh as the mechanism to deliver /rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token into the container but never declares the env-var name the token maps to, does not specify that fetch-secrets.sh will be updated with the new SSM leaf, and does not confirm the autolander service reads the correct env-var — leaving the SSM-leaf → env-var → container process binding entirely undeclared on the secret-delivery path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'fetch-secrets.sh is updated to add: `autolander-gerrit-token` → `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN` in the required (non-optional) mapping; Dockerfile.autolander reads `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN` from the container env; `.env` is never committed.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` is a 'server-level capability granted in `All-Projects` `refs/meta/config` (`[capability]` / service-user grant)' but no `All-Projects` config file exists anywhere in `infra/gerrit/` (only the `rebar` project's `project.config` is present). This grant is not represented in any IaC or setup script, making the assertion unverifiable and the work potentially invisible — nothing downstream provisions it.
findings.location: AC2 — '`autolander-bot`/RebarBotNava's `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` is a server-level capability granted in `All-Projects` `refs/meta/config` (`[capability]` / service-user grant)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC4: Resolve the 'autoheal/`restart: unless-stopped`' ambiguity — either commit to adding a `willfarrell/autoheal` (or equivalent) sidecar service to docker-compose.yml (scoped to the autolander container by label) or specify the concrete alternative mechanism that restarts the container on `unhealthy` state, not just on process exit.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'autoheal/`restart: unless-stopped`' as the mechanism to recreate the container on `unhealthy` state (AC4), but `restart: unless-stopped` only fires on process EXIT — the plan itself says so. The `willfarrell/autoheal` container is the established prior-art sidecar for restarting `unhealthy` containers in plain docker compose, but the plan neither commits to adding it to docker-compose.yml nor considers alternatives (e.g., a host-side systemd unit watching `docker inspect` health, or Docker 25+'s `restart on-failure` extension). Leaving 'autoheal/restart' as a slash-ambiguity means an implementer may ship only `restart: unless-stopped` and silently miss the unhealthy-restart requirement — breaking AC3's 'unhealthy state feeds the metric' loop.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an autoheal sidecar to the compose service spec: `image: willfarrell/autoheal:latest`, `environment: AUTOHEAL_CONTAINER_LABEL=autoheal`, `volumes: /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock`, and label the autolander service with `autoheal: 'true'`. Or, if Docker 25+ restart policies are available on the AL2023 box, specify `restart: on-failure` with a health-check dependency — but verify the Docker version first.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the autoheal mechanism concretely in AC4: either name the autoheal image and its required compose service definition (socket mount, `AUTOHEAL_CONTAINER_LABEL` env, `restart: unless-stopped`), or name an alternative (e.g., a systemd `ExecStartPost` watcher using `docker events`) — do not leave 'autoheal/' as an unresolved option.
findings.finding: The autoheal/restart-on-unhealthy mechanism is named in AC4 as 'autoheal/restart: unless-stopped' but left unspecified. `restart: unless-stopped` in plain docker compose only restarts on container EXIT, not on HEALTHCHECK `unhealthy` status. The plan acknowledges this ('a plain `restart: unless-stopped` only fires on process EXIT, not a hang') but then lists 'autoheal' as if it will be wired in, without specifying which autoheal implementation (e.g., `willfarrell/autoheal` image), how it is added to docker-compose.yml, or how it accesses the Docker socket. If only `restart: unless-stopped` is wired (without an autoheal sidecar), hangs are not recovered.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) — 'the compose service + an autoheal/`restart: unless-stopped` recreate it on exit/unhealthy'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC4: Specify the HEALTHCHECK command (e.g. a heartbeat-file mtime sentinel or TCP probe), `--interval`, `--timeout`, and `--retries` so the executor does not have to choose the detection mechanism.
findings.finding: AC4 specifies that the HEALTHCHECK 'marks the container unhealthy on a hang' but does not specify the healthcheck command, interval, or timeout — an executor must choose an appropriate liveness probe, which is a real design decision (file-mtime sentinel, TCP probe, heartbeat script, etc.).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'HEALTHCHECK --interval=60s --timeout=10s --retries=3 CMD test $(( $(date +%s) - $(stat -c %Y /state/heartbeat) )) -lt 180 (the loop touches /state/heartbeat each cycle; S2 must emit this touch)' — or whichever sentinel S2's loop already produces.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a named volume (e.g., `autolander_state`) in the compose service's `volumes:` stanza and document its CREATE/RETIRE lifecycle so the flock lockfile is reliably shared across all container instances and cleaned up on decommission.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'a lockfile in its state volume' as the single-instance guard but does not specify whether that volume is a named (persistent) Docker volume or an anonymous one. With an anonymous volume, each container recreation spawns a fresh volume mount, meaning two simultaneously-starting containers (e.g., from an external trigger while a restart is in flight) would each see their own empty volume and both successfully acquire the lock — defeating the guard entirely.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Single-instance guard (AC2 mechanism bullet) and Dockerfile.autolander / docker-compose.yml
findings.suggested_fix: In `docker-compose.yml`, add a top-level named volume `autolander_state` and mount it at a fixed path (e.g., `/var/run/autolander`) in the autolander service; reference this volume name in the AC so implementers know it must be persistent and shared. Document that `docker volume rm autolander_state` is the decommission step.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC1: Specify that the autolander's state volume is declared `external: true` in `docker-compose.yml` and is pre-created by `compose-up.sh` (as a bind mount onto the EBS-backed host path), matching the pattern used for all other stateful volumes.
findings.finding: The plan says the flock lockfile lives 'in its state volume' but does not specify whether this volume is declared `external: true` in `docker-compose.yml`. Every other stateful volume in the compose stack is `external: true` (preventing `docker compose down -v` destruction); if the autolander's state volume is a regular named volume without `external: true`, a `docker compose down -v` during a deploy cycle silently removes it, which (though not a data-loss event for business data) removes the lock state and could cause dual-start confusion.
findings.location: AC1 (Dockerfile.autolander + compose service) — 'a lockfile in its state volume'; infra/compose/docker-compose.yml volumes section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'The autolander state volume (`autolander_state`) is declared `external: true` and bind-mounted onto `/var/gerrit/site/autolander` (EBS-backed); `compose-up.sh` creates it alongside the other external volumes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) pin the compose project name explicitly (`name: rebar` at the top of docker-compose.yml or `COMPOSE_PROJECT_NAME=rebar` in an `.env` file) and document the expected container name, OR (b) make observability.sh derive the container name dynamically (`docker compose ps -q autolander | xargs docker inspect --format …`) rather than hardcoding it — and add a startup self-check that logs a warning if zero matching containers are found.
findings.finding: The `CONTAINER_NAME=compose-autolander-1` grep in observability.sh is a hardcoded assertion about Docker Compose auto-naming: the container name is `{project}-{service}-{instance}` where project defaults to the working-directory name (assumed here to be 'compose'). If the compose project name differs (e.g., invoked as `docker compose -p rebar up`, or if the directory is renamed), the unhealthy-state feeder silently produces zero matches — no metric fires, no error is raised, and operators have no indication the second feeder is broken.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `name: rebar` (or another fixed token) as the top-level `name:` field in docker-compose.yml (Compose spec ≥ 2.0 supports this), making the container name deterministic (`rebar-autolander-1`). Update the grep pattern to match, and add a guard in observability.sh: `if [[ $(docker ps --filter name=rebar-autolander-1 --format '{{.Names}}' | wc -l) -eq 0 ]]; then logger -t observability 'WARNING: autolander container not found — unhealthy feeder disabled'; fi`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to the title or Scope section acknowledging that AC2 includes Gerrit server-level capability provisioning (a distinct admin step), OR explicitly defer it to a prerequisite story, so the implementing engineer's scope is unambiguous.
findings.finding: AC2 introduces substantial Gerrit server-level admin configuration (granting `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` as a server-level capability in `All-Projects` refs/meta/config, plus submit permission on `refs/heads/*`) that is absent from the title and represents a distinct actor/concern (Gerrit administrator action vs. infra deploy engineer). This is undeclared scope relative to the title 'Deploy (container + compose, single instance) + observability'.
findings.location: AC2 (Credentials mechanism) — 'rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader is a server-level capability granted in All-Projects refs/meta/config'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the Scope line: 'Includes one-time Gerrit admin step: grant rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader and submit on refs/heads/* to autolander-bot in All-Projects (AC2).' This makes the extra scope visible without splitting the story.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit verification criteria for each new behavior: (1) a test/script that starts two containers and asserts the second exits non-zero (flock guard); (2) a smoke test or CI step that injects an AUTOLANDER_ERROR log line and asserts the metric is published; (3) a test that the container transitions to `unhealthy` when the healthcheck command fails; (4) a test that `fetch-secrets.sh` fails fast and logs clearly when the SSM path is absent.
findings.finding: The plan introduces several new testable behaviors — the flock-based single-instance guard (explicitly the SOLE mechanism), the two-feeder observability script, the HEALTHCHECK hang-detection, and SSM credential fetching — but specifies no tests, verification steps, or acceptance evidence for any of them. The flock guard in particular has no stated happy-path test (second copy exits) or failure-path test (lock held across restarts, volume not mounted, stale lockfile). The observability feeders have no stated test that either the `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` journald marker or the `unhealthy` container state actually triggers the `autolander_errors` metric.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC (flock guard), AC3 (observability), AC4 (healthcheck)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Testing / Verification' section to the plan with: (a) a local `docker compose` test script that launches two containers sharing the lock volume and asserts the second exits with code 1 within N seconds; (b) a CI-runnable unit test for `observability.sh` that mocks `journalctl` output containing AUTOLANDER_ERROR and `docker inspect` output showing `unhealthy`, then asserts `aws cloudwatch put-metric-data` is called with the correct metric name; (c) an AC line stating 'the Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK is validated by running the container without the expected process and confirming `docker inspect` shows Status=unhealthy within 90 s'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` to the plan's edit-target list, specifying that `SITE_SUBDIRS` must be extended with the autolander state subdir (e.g. `autolander`) to satisfy the config-check.sh check 5 drift gate.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` is not enumerated as an edit target, but adding the autolander's state volume as `external: true` in `docker-compose.yml` will immediately fail the `config-check.sh` check 5 drift gate unless `SITE_SUBDIRS` in `compose-up.sh` is extended to include the new volume — this is an enforced CI invariant, not a soft guideline.
findings.location: AC1 (single-instance guard): 'a lockfile in its state volume'; AC1 (Dockerfile + compose service in docker-compose.yml)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'Edit `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh`: append `autolander` (or the chosen subdir name) to `SITE_SUBDIRS` so the external volume is provisioned at boot and the CI drift gate passes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` to the plan's edit-target list, specifying that `BOT_PATHS` (or an equivalent `AUTOLANDER_PATHS` variable) must include `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` and the autolander service name so source changes trigger an auto-redeploy.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh`'s `BOT_PATHS` variable controls which source-file changes trigger an autolander container rebuild; `Dockerfile.autolander` (and any autolander-specific source directory outside `src/rebar/`) is not currently in `BOT_PATHS`, so source changes to the autolander service would not trigger auto-redeploy. The plan does not enumerate `autodeploy.sh` as an edit target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC2: Resolve the `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` capability assertion by citing the Gerrit 3.14.1 docs (or a live `gerrit ls-groups`/`gerrit show-account` verification) confirming the exact `[capability]` stanza key name, and clarify whether `forgeAuthor`/`forgeCommitter` must also be granted (as flagged in prior S2 plan reviews) for DCO trailers to be preserved.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` is a server-level global capability grantable in `All-Projects` refs/meta/config `[capability]` section, but cites no Gerrit version reference or documentation anchor, and no existing infra file in the repo provisions or exercises this key. Prior review-cache entries for related stories (S2/S1 epics) repeatedly flagged this same capability as unverified. An unresolved capability assertion here is load-bearing: if the key name is wrong, or if the Gerrit 3.14.1 instance requires a different stanza or group-membership model, the bot cannot rebase on behalf of uploaders and the S2 loop breaks.
findings.location: AC2 / Credentials mechanism — 'rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader is a server-level capability granted in All-Projects refs/meta/config ([capability] / service-user grant)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Verification' section (or per-AC proving commands) covering at minimum: `docker compose build autolander && docker compose up -d autolander`, `aws ssm get-parameter --name /rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token --with-decryption`, a simulated `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` log line triggering the metric script, and a second-container lock-acquisition test (`docker compose run autolander` exits non-zero while first is running).
findings.finding: No AC contains a concrete proving command that produces observable evidence of completion; the plan describes what to build but never how to verify it is done, violating the E6 requirement that every completion-relevant claim has a concrete proving command.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for the flock guard: start the container, then run `docker compose run --rm autolander` (or equivalent) and assert it exits with a non-zero code, e.g. `docker run ... autolander; [ $? -ne 0 ]`.
findings.finding: The single-instance flock guard AC has no proving command — there is no described test that launches a second container instance while the first holds the lock and asserts the second exits non-zero, so the guard's correctness is unverifiable in-session.
findings.suggested_fix: Proving command: `docker compose up -d autolander && sleep 5 && docker compose run --rm autolander; echo "second exit: $?"` — assert exit code is non-zero (lock held).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an end-to-end observability proving step: inject a synthetic `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` log line into the container's stdout/journald, run `observability.sh`, and assert a data point appears in CloudWatch via `aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics --namespace rebar/host --metric-name autolander_errors ...`.
findings.finding: AC3's observability pipeline has no end-to-end proving command that exercises the live path: the plan describes the metric being published from two feeders but provides no command to inject a real `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` journald marker or a real `unhealthy` container state and observe the resulting CloudWatch `PutMetricData` call, leaving the cutover path unexercised.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign an explicit AC number to the single-instance guard bullet (e.g. relabel it 'Single-instance guard (AC2)' and shift credentials to 'AC3', observability to 'AC4', liveness to 'AC5') so positional and labeled numbering agree throughout.
findings.finding: The credentials bullet is explicitly labeled '(AC2 mechanism)' but is the third bullet point in the AC list; the second bullet (single-instance guard) carries no AC number. This creates an ambiguous referencing scheme: 'AC2' could be interpreted as the second listed bullet (single-instance guard) or the credential bullet (which self-labels as AC2). AC4 references 'the same autolander_errors metric named in AC3' — a cross-AC reference that works — but if any downstream review or test plan references 'AC2' to mean the single-instance guard (positional reading), it will collide with the credential bullet's self-label.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — second bullet ('Single-instance guard') and third bullet ('Credentials (AC2 mechanism)')
findings.suggested_fix: Relabel bullets sequentially: AC1 = Dockerfile+compose, AC2 = Single-instance guard, AC3 = Credentials, AC4 = Observability, AC5 = Liveness/restart. Update all internal cross-references (e.g. 'the same autolander_errors metric named in AC4') accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC2: declare the Gerrit base URL (and protocol) the autolander uses — e.g. 'the autolander calls `http://localhost:8080` (loopback-only, same host, no cross-network hop)' or 'calls the public HTTPS URL' — so the token's transit posture is explicit.
findings.finding: The plan does not declare the access posture of the autolander container's outbound connection to Gerrit — specifically whether it calls `http://localhost:8080` (loopback HTTP, token in Authorization header) or `https://...` (TLS). If the token is sent over loopback HTTP it is not encrypted in transit; while loopback reduces blast radius, the posture is undeclared, which violates the plan's own requirement that every new data path declare its access class.
findings.location: AC2 (Credentials mechanism) — entire section; no Gerrit base URL or protocol declared
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'The autolander calls Gerrit at `http://localhost:8080` (loopback, same Docker bridge as the Gerrit container; the token never crosses an untrusted network segment). The base URL is injected as `GERRIT_BASE_URL=http://localhost:8080` in the container environment.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts the autolander Gerrit HTTP token is provisioned at SSM path `/rebar/autolander/gerrit-http-token` and 'fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`', but fetch-secrets.sh hardcodes `SSM_PREFIX="/rebar/prod"` and only fetches from that prefix. A secret at `/rebar/autolander/gerrit-http-token` would NOT be fetched by the existing script without modification, and the path breaks the established `/rebar/prod/*` naming convention used in ssm.tf.
findings.location: AC2 — 'provisioned as an SSM SecureString at the concrete path `/rebar/autolander/gerrit-http-token` (added in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`, fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the stated SSM path to `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-http-token` to match the hardcoded prefix in `fetch-secrets.sh`, add it to the `rebar_secret_params` list in `ssm.tf`, and add a `get_param autolander-gerrit-http-token` call + `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_HTTP_TOKEN=...` line to `fetch-secrets.sh`.

findings.finding: The SSM path `/rebar/autolander/gerrit-http-token` specified in AC2 breaks the repo-wide `/rebar/prod/*` prefix convention established in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` and consumed uniformly by `fetch-secrets.sh`. No existing SSM parameter uses the `/rebar/autolander/` prefix.
findings.location: AC2: 'provisioned as an SSM SecureString at the concrete path `/rebar/autolander/gerrit-http-token`'
findings.suggested_fix: Use path `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-http-token` to align with `SSM_PREFIX=/rebar/prod` in `fetch-secrets.sh` and the existing `ssm.tf` resource block.

findings.finding: The SSM path `/rebar/autolander/gerrit-http-token` specified in AC2 falls outside the existing EC2 instance-role IAM grant (`arn:aws:ssm:...parameter/rebar/prod/*` in `iam.tf`). `fetch-secrets.sh` would fail to read it at runtime — the token would be unfetchable — unless either the path is corrected to `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-http-token` (matching the existing prefix) or a new IAM grant is added for `/rebar/autolander/*`.
findings.suggested_fix: Use path `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-http-token` to stay within the existing `parameter/rebar/prod/*` IAM grant, matching the convention of all other secrets (`ssm.tf`, `fetch-secrets.sh`).

findings.finding: The plan specifies the Gerrit HTTP token at SSM path `/rebar/autolander/gerrit-http-token`, but the existing IAM role policy (`iam.tf` `ssm_params_read`) scopes `ssm:GetParameter` to `arn:aws:ssm:…:parameter/rebar/prod/*`. The `/rebar/autolander/` prefix is outside that wildcard, so any on-box reader (fetch-secrets.sh or the container) will receive `AccessDeniedException` at runtime. The plan's claim that this path is 'fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`' is an unverified capability assertion — fetch-secrets.sh only knows the `/rebar/prod` prefix.
findings.location: AC2 — 'provisioned as an SSM SecureString at the concrete path `/rebar/autolander/gerrit-http-token` (added in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`, fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use the path `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-http-token` to stay within the existing `/rebar/prod/*` IAM scope and the established SSM_PREFIX in fetch-secrets.sh. Add it to the `rebar_secret_params` list in `ssm.tf` and a `get_param autolander-gerrit-http-token` call in `fetch-secrets.sh`, mirroring how `/rebar/prod/gerrit-bot-token` is handled for the review-bot.

findings.finding: The plan's AC2 provisions the Gerrit HTTP token at SSM path `/rebar/autolander/gerrit-http-token` — a NEW namespace that falls outside the existing IAM grant, which scopes the instance role's SSM reads to `parameter/rebar/prod/*` only (iam.tf line 59). Fetching a param under `/rebar/autolander/` would fail at runtime without an additional IAM policy attachment not mentioned in the plan. This is also config-surface proliferation: the codebase has one SSM namespace convention (`/rebar/prod/`) enforced by IAM; introducing a second namespace (`/rebar/autolander/`) without updating IAM or explaining the divergence creates two parallel conventions.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-http-token` — add it to the `rebar_secret_params` list in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` and to `fetch-secrets.sh`'s mapping, following the exact pattern used for `/rebar/prod/gerrit-bot-token` (the review-bot's equivalent credential). No new IAM is needed.

findings.finding: The new SSM parameter path `/rebar/autolander/gerrit-http-token` falls outside the existing instance-role IAM policy's scope (`arn:aws:ssm:…:parameter/rebar/prod/*`), so the instance role cannot read it. The plan specifies the SSM path and its fetch in `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`, but never mentions adding an IAM policy statement (or inline policy on the instance role) to allow `ssm:GetParameter` + `kms:Decrypt` for the `/rebar/autolander/*` prefix.
findings.location: AC — Credentials (AC2 mechanism): `/rebar/autolander/gerrit-http-token` added in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`, fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`
findings.suggested_fix: Add `infra/terraform/iam_s5_autolander.tf` with an `aws_iam_role_policy` on `data.aws_iam_role.instance` (already declared in `iam_s2.tf`) that grants `ssm:GetParameter` + `ssm:GetParameters` on `arn:aws:ssm:${var.aws_region}:${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}:parameter/rebar/autolander/*` and `kms:Decrypt` with `kms:ViaService = ssm.${var.aws_region}.amazonaws.com` — mirroring the pattern in `iam.tf` lines 50-78. Alternatively, move the token path to `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-http-token` to fall under the already-granted prefix (and update `ssm.tf` + `fetch-secrets.sh` accordingly).

findings.finding: The plan places the autolander Gerrit HTTP token at SSM path `/rebar/autolander/gerrit-http-token`, but the existing IAM policy (`iam.tf`, resource `rebar-gerrit-ssm-params-read`) scopes `ssm:GetParameter` to `arn:aws:ssm:...:parameter/rebar/prod/*`. A parameter under `/rebar/autolander/` is outside that prefix, so the EC2 instance role cannot read it at runtime — the integration is contradicted by existing IAM configuration.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) change the SSM path to `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-http-token` (stays under the existing `rebar/prod/*` IAM scope, no IAM change needed) and update ssm.tf + fetch-secrets.sh accordingly, or (b) keep `/rebar/autolander/gerrit-http-token` and add a second resource ARN `parameter/rebar/autolander/*` to the `ssm_params_read` policy document in iam.tf.

findings.finding: The plan places the autolander Gerrit HTTP token at SSM path `/rebar/autolander/gerrit-http-token`, but the EC2 instance role's `ssm:GetParameter` is scoped exclusively to `arn:...:parameter/rebar/prod/*` (iam.tf line 59). The `/rebar/autolander/` prefix is outside that scope — the runtime fetch will receive IAM AccessDenied, leaving the container unable to authenticate to Gerrit.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the SSM parameter to `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-http-token` (consistent with every other bot token, e.g. `/rebar/prod/gerrit-bot-token`). This requires no IAM change, keeps `ssm.tf`'s `rebar_secret_params` list as the single source, and aligns with the existing `fetch-secrets.sh` prefix convention.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
